title: INR Calculator – International Normalized Ratio
tagline: Calculate your International Normalized Ratio (INR) from prothrombin time measurements to monitor anticoagulant therapy and assess blood clotting function.
intro: Enter your prothrombin time, control PT, and International Sensitivity Index to instantly compute your INR and see how it compares to clinical reference ranges.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for all required fields.
error.isiRange: ISI is typically between 0.9 and 2.0. Please verify your value.

result.heading: Your INR Result
result.formula: Formula: INR = (Patient PT ÷ Control PT) ^ ISI

about.heading: About the INR Calculator
about.body:
The International Normalized Ratio (INR) is a standardized measure of how long it takes your blood to clot, expressed relative to a normal reference. Before INR was introduced, laboratories reported prothrombin time (PT) in raw seconds, which varied dramatically between institutions because each lab used a different thromboplastin reagent. A patient's PT of 18 seconds in one hospital might indicate the same clotting speed as 24 seconds in another, making it impossible to compare results or safely transfer patients. The INR was created by the World Health Organization to solve this problem by anchoring every result to a universal standard.

The calculation relies on two measurements collected in the same blood draw. The patient's PT is the time, in seconds, for the patient's plasma to form a clot when thromboplastin and calcium are added. The control PT is the clotting time of a normal reference plasma tested simultaneously under identical conditions. The ratio of patient PT to control PT would already give useful information, but different reagents still respond to clotting factor deficiencies at different sensitivities. The International Sensitivity Index (ISI) corrects for this: it is a calibration value assigned to each thromboplastin lot by comparing it against the World Health Organization's international reference preparation. The final formula is INR = (patient PT / control PT) raised to the power of the ISI.

For a healthy adult not taking anticoagulant medication, the INR falls between 0.8 and 1.2. Anything above 1.2 represents slower clotting than normal. For patients on warfarin therapy for conditions such as atrial fibrillation, deep-vein thrombosis, or pulmonary embolism, clinicians typically target an INR of 2.0 to 3.0. Patients with mechanical heart valves, which create a higher risk of thrombus formation, often require a higher therapeutic target of 2.5 to 3.5. An INR above 4.5 signals significantly elevated bleeding risk and usually requires clinical intervention — holding the warfarin dose, administering vitamin K, or in emergencies using fresh frozen plasma or prothrombin complex concentrates.

Accurate INR testing depends on proper pre-analytical steps. Blood must be drawn into a 3.2 percent sodium citrate tube in a precise 9-to-1 blood-to-anticoagulant ratio; an under- or over-filled tube distorts the result. Samples should be processed within four hours of collection, and the patient and control plasma must be tested simultaneously. Factors that can acutely affect INR include new medications (especially antibiotics and antifungals that alter gut flora responsible for vitamin K production), changes in dietary vitamin K intake such as sudden increases in leafy green vegetables, illness, alcohol consumption, and changes in weight or body composition.

This INR calculator is intended as an educational and reference tool. Always confirm your INR with laboratory testing and consult your prescribing physician or anticoagulation clinic before adjusting any medication dose. The interpretation ranges shown reflect common clinical guidelines but individual patient targets may differ.

examples.heading: INR Calculation Examples
examples.intro: Four worked scenarios illustrating normal, therapeutic, and elevated INR values across typical clinical situations.

items.note: Normal healthy individual not on anticoagulation. PT ratio = 1.0; INR = 1.0^1.0 = 1.0. Within normal range 0.8–1.2.
Therapeutic range for warfarin patient with atrial fibrillation. PT ratio = 2.0; INR = 2.0^1.0 = 2.0. Target range 2.0–3.0.
Upper end of standard therapeutic range. Appropriate for some high-risk venous thromboembolism patients. Monitor closely.
Supratherapeutic. High bleeding risk. Clinical review and likely dose reduction required immediately.

howto.heading: How to Use the INR Calculator
steps: Select your PT input mode: enter the raw Patient PT and Control PT in seconds, or enter a pre-calculated PT ratio if your lab already reports it.
Type in your Prothrombin Time (Patient PT) and Control PT values from your lab report, both in seconds.
Enter the International Sensitivity Index (ISI) for the thromboplastin reagent used — this is printed on your lab report or available from your laboratory; a common default is 1.0.
Click Calculate INR to see your result, the PT ratio, and an interpretation based on standard clinical reference ranges.
Share the result with your anticoagulation clinic or physician — do not adjust your warfarin dose based on this calculator alone.

items.q: What is a normal INR value?
items.a: For a healthy adult who is not taking any anticoagulant medication, a normal INR is between 0.8 and 1.2. Values in this range indicate that blood is clotting within a typical time frame. An INR below 0.8 is rare but can occur with certain clotting factor elevations; an INR above 1.2 indicates some degree of anticoagulation or a clotting factor deficiency.

items.q: What INR range is therapeutic for warfarin patients?
items.a: The standard therapeutic INR target for most patients on warfarin — including those with atrial fibrillation, deep-vein thrombosis, or pulmonary embolism — is 2.0 to 3.0. Patients with mechanical heart valves typically require a higher target of 2.5 to 3.5 because of the greater risk of valve-related thrombosis. Your prescriber will set an individualized target based on your specific condition and bleeding risk.

items.q: What does the ISI value represent?
items.a: The International Sensitivity Index (ISI) is a calibration constant that describes how sensitive a particular thromboplastin reagent is relative to the WHO international reference preparation. A reagent with an ISI of 1.0 behaves identically to the reference. Most commercial reagents have ISI values between 0.9 and 1.7. Your lab report should include the ISI, or you can ask your laboratory directly.

items.q: What happens if my INR is too high?
items.a: An INR above 3.0–3.5 (or above 4.5 for non-valve patients) indicates over-anticoagulation with an elevated risk of bleeding. Clinicians typically hold one or more warfarin doses, may administer oral vitamin K to reverse the effect, and increase monitoring frequency. In cases of active bleeding or an INR above 10, more aggressive reversal with intravenous vitamin K, fresh frozen plasma, or prothrombin complex concentrates may be required.

items.q: How often should I check my INR?
items.a: Monitoring frequency depends on how stable your INR has been. Stable patients who have been in range consistently for months may only need to test every four to six weeks. After a dose change, illness, new medication, or significant dietary change, retesting within one to two weeks is common. Patients with persistently unstable INR values may be tested weekly. Your anticoagulation clinic or physician will advise you on the right interval for your situation.

Yes — dietary vitamin K is the most significant food-related variable. Vitamin K is found in high amounts in leafy green vegetables such as spinach, kale, broccoli, and Brussels sprouts. Because vitamin K is required for the synthesis of several clotting factors, a sudden increase in intake lowers INR, while a sudden decrease raises it. The key is consistency rather than avoidance — eating a steady amount of vitamin K-rich foods allows your warfarin dose to be calibrated around your usual diet.
